content: Imagine you are building a high-performance text editor in Rust where the core logic is a recursive spell-checker that traverses millions of lines of code. You start with a naive approach using std::string::String everywhere. The first failure point is memory fragmentation: every time your spell checker encounters a new identifier, it allocates heap space, copies data, and potentially deallocates if the buffer grows. This allocation overhead becomes a bottleneck under load. To fix this, you implement small-string optimization by ensuring that identifiers shorter than a threshold are stored directly on the stack rather than requiring a heap pointer. You choose SmolStr for your identifier parsing logic because it is designed specifically for short strings and supports traits like DerefMut to integrate seamlessly with existing string APIs without breaking your codebase. As you refine the system, you discover that even with small-string optimization, the memory usage for medium-length identifiers remains high, creating a ceiling on performance. The correct approach now shifts to adopting CompactString as a drop-in replacement for String in your hot paths where you need zero-copy semantics and automatic growth without allocation spikes. CompactString uses a compact representation that stores shorter strings inline and grows efficiently by doubling capacity when needed, which eliminates the fragmentation problem entirely. You replace every instance of std::string::String with CompactString in your data structures and pass it through your trait implementations since it implements all necessary traits including Clone and Display without any runtime overhead. The final result is a system where memory allocation only occurs when absolutely necessary for large identifiers, allowing your spell checker to process millions of lines per second with consistent latency. You now have a robust solution that combines the best of both worlds: SmolStr for strict identifier handling and CompactString as the general-purpose string type that removes the performance ceiling previously imposed by heap allocations.
